method2.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island's Japanese name is Hashima (端島).[2] It is also widely known as Gunkanjima (軍艦島), commonly translated into English as “Battleship Island”.[2] The nickname combines gunkan, meaning “warship,” and jima, meaning “island”.[2] The name arose from the island’s appearance, which was compared to a battleship when seen from a distance.[2] In 1916, the Osaka Asahi Shimbun reported that Hashima resembled a giant battleship with two chimneys.[4] In 1921, the Nagasaki Nichinichi Shimbun coined the name Gunkanjima after noting the island’s resemblance to the battleship Tosa.[4]

==History==
Hashima Island developed from a small coal-mining and fishing site into a major undersea coal-mining settlement off Nagasaki.[1][3] Coal had long been known in the surrounding area, with people on nearby Takashima reportedly collecting exposed coal seams for household fuel before industrial mining expanded.[2] During Japan’s Meiji-era industrialization, Hashima became part of a broader shift toward coal-powered modernization, linking the island’s history to changes in national energy policy.[2]  

Mitsubishi transformed Hashima into a company town, expanding the island with landfill, enclosing it with seawalls, and concentrating mining, housing, services, and daily life within a very small area.[4] By 1916, Hashima was producing about 150,000 tons of coal annually and had a population of more than 3,000.[2] In the same year, Mitsubishi built a large reinforced-concrete apartment block on the island to address overcrowding and reduce typhoon damage, a structure described as Japan’s first concrete building of significant size.[2] Over subsequent decades, concrete apartment blocks, schools, hospitals, and entertainment facilities were built to support the growing mining population.[5]  

Hashima’s dense built environment gave rise to its nickname Gunkanjima, or “Battleship Island,” because its seawalls and concrete buildings made it resemble a warship at sea.[1][3] At its postwar peak in 1959, the island reached an estimated population density of 83,500 people per square kilometer, with the residential district approaching 139,100 people per square kilometer.[3] By the 1950s, more than 5,000 residents lived on the island’s roughly 16 acres, making Hashima one of the most densely populated places in the world.[5]  

The island’s history also includes coercive wartime labor under Japan’s imperial labor system, including forced labor connected to the mine.[3][4] Scholars and heritage critics have emphasized that Hashima was one part of a wider imperial labor regime shaped by colonial status, policing, wage discipline, confinement, and unequal ability to leave.[4] Later memories of Hashima as a close-knit postwar community coexist with these earlier histories of coercion, making the island a contested site of interpretation.[4]  

Hashima’s prosperity declined as Japan’s energy economy shifted away from coal and toward petroleum in the 1960s.[5] Mitsubishi closed the mine in 1974, and the island was abandoned soon afterward as residents left its apartments, schools, shops, and public spaces behind.[5] In the decades after abandonment, Hashima’s concrete buildings deteriorated under exposure to typhoons, waves, wind, and fire, turning the former mining town into a prominent urban ruin.[1][5] The island later became accessible by boat from Nagasaki and gained attention as both a tourist destination and a symbol of Japan’s industrial past.[1][5]  

Hashima forms part of the UNESCO-listed “Sites of Japan’s Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining,” but its listing and interpretation have generated diplomatic tension over how to represent wartime labor history.[1][3]

==Current status==
Hashima Island has been uninhabited since 1974, when its residents left after nearly a century of undersea coal mining.[5] Its present landscape consists of abandoned high-rise buildings, a man-made seawall, a shrine on a rocky cliff, and little vegetation.[5] The island is commonly known as Gunkanjima, or “Battleship Island”, because of its distinctive silhouette.[2][5] In 2015, it was inscribed as part of the UNESCO World Heritage listing for the Sites of Japan’s Meiji Industrial Revolution.[1][2] The site’s heritage interpretation remains disputed, particularly over Japan’s presentation of Korean wartime labor at the former coal mine.[1] At the 47th session of the UNESCO World Heritage Committee, the committee backed Japan’s position that the dispute should be handled through bilateral dialogue rather than further UNESCO procedures.[1] Hashima also continues to circulate in tourism, popular culture, and digital media as an iconic ruined industrial landscape, including through fictional depictions and immersive or virtual-reality projects.[4]

==Access==
Hashima Island lies about 15 km off Nagasaki and is accessible to the public only through authorized guided boat tours.[2] Most tours depart from Nagasaki Port, which can be reached from Nagasaki Eki-mae Station by a short tram ride to Ohato Station, or from Nagasaki Airport by bus to Ohato Station.[1] Landings are subject to Nagasaki City safety regulations requiring wave heights below 0.5 m, wind speeds below 5 m/s, and visibility above 500 m.[1] Even when these conditions are met, the boat captain may cancel landing if conditions are judged unsafe, and boats are able to land on only about 100 days per year.[1] Visitors who land on the island are accompanied by guides and must remain on designated viewing routes.[4] Access to the apartment blocks, mine structures, and other ruins is restricted, so tours focus mainly on exterior viewing from controlled routes.[2] On busy days, visitor numbers can exceed 1,400 per day, with about 260 visitors passing through per hour, and guides manage crowding along the routes.[4]

==World Heritage Site approval controversy==
Hashima Island was included among the 23 components of Japan's “Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution,” a World Heritage nomination intended to represent Japan's rapid transformation into an industrial power in the late 19th century.[2] UNESCO's World Heritage Committee designated the group in 2015, and Hashima became part of the World Heritage site despite objections centered on wartime labor history.[3][5] South Korea objected that seven of the nominated industrial sites, including Hashima, had used forced labor during World War II and argued that those sites should be removed from the bid.[1] Korean accounts cited Hashima as the most notorious of the disputed locations, stating that about 600 Koreans worked in its undersea coal mines and that many were believed to have died from exposure or overwork.[1] The dispute occurred amid broader tensions over Japan's public treatment of its imperial and wartime history, including disagreements over “comfort women” and school textbook interpretations.[1] Critics in South Korea and elsewhere argued that World Heritage recognition was not inherently inappropriate, but that Hashima should be interpreted in a way that acknowledged coerced labor and the darker aspects of the island's industrial past.[5] Supporters of the Japanese nomination emphasized the Meiji-era period from 1850 to 1910 and argued that the sites' primary value lay in their role in Japan's industrialization rather than in the 1940s wartime labor period.[2][3] After the 2015 approval, controversy continued around Japan's Industrial Heritage Information Centre, whose displays South Korea said gave insufficient explanation of people from the former Korean Peninsula.[3] In 2020, South Korean Foreign Minister Kang Kyung-wha asked UNESCO to consider removing Hashima's World Heritage status and requested greater cooperation from Japan.[3] UNESCO subsequently planned discussions with Japan and an inspection of the information center, while Japanese representatives maintained that UNESCO should assess the issue using accurate information and with regard to Japan's position as a sovereign state.[3]

==In popular culture==
Hashima Island has been widely represented through fictional and mediated images, with James Bond fans recognizing it as Raoul Silva’s lair in the 2012 film *Skyfall*.[2][4] The island has also appeared in National Geographic’s *One Strange Rock*, alongside coverage emphasizing its abandoned concrete landscape and decay.[4] Survival-horror games and immersive media have used or evoked Hashima, including *The Closed Ward* and later virtual-reality projects, museum installations, VRChat worlds, and commercial walking simulators.[2] Tourism campaigns and the island’s 2015 UNESCO World Heritage listing have further circulated images of its ruins as picturesque decay, helping make Gunkanjima a major tourist attraction in Nagasaki.[2][3] Scholars have argued that such popular representations can shape collective memory of Hashima and affect debates over Japan’s responsibility for wartime forced labor and other abuses associated with the island.[1] Critical studies of *Skyfall* and related visual culture contend that entertainment-focused portrayals may reinforce a triumphalist view of Japan’s industrial heritage while obscuring histories of trauma, loss, and reparation.[1]
